The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope processes, were identified by their charac- teristic opaque, chaotic, and discontinuous seismic facies. MTDs, commonly referred to as submarine landslides, occur on submarine slopes and typically involve the downslope movement of materials, sometimes over considerable distances and volumes, into low-gradient areas (Hampton et al., 1996; Owen et al., 2007). Due to their high mobility, MTDs exhibit significant variability in seismic signatures (Mohrig et al., 1998; Gee et al., 1999). To characterize the MTDs, we first analyzed the geomorphological features and internal architecture, following the framework established by Moscardelli and Wood (2008). In particular, there is signiﬁcant less knowledge about the critical condi- tions under which a slope failure will occur. Most published data con- cerns ancient slope failures, with their formation mechanisms and triggers being hard to estimate. In addition, it is diﬃcult to predict when and where a slope failure will occur in the future, and there is an overall lack of in-situ measurements in the areas where recent sub- marine landslides have occurred. This study suggests that slope sediments can fail under their own gravity when gravitational forces along the slope overcome the shear strength of sediments.
